result:
Okay, here I go. I've always found it fascinating how easily we adapt to the absurd. Think about tying a piece of fabric around your face all day – a couple of years ago, that would have been considered quite eccentric. Now, it's commonplace, almost unremarkable. We barely bat an eye.

This adaptability extends beyond just protective measures. We've embraced an entirely digital existence, holding conversations with disembodied voices and receiving curated algorithms telling us what to buy and watch. Are we even really thinking for ourselves anymore? Are we just reacting to stimuli, constantly stimulated and never truly bored?

I worry about that. I worry about the loss of quiet moments, the moments where our thoughts drift and wander, unprompted. Those moments used to be the breeding ground for creativity, for innovation, for simply understanding ourselves. Now, it feels like those moments are actively being crowded out by the incessant demands of our devices.

Maybe I'm just old-fashioned. Maybe this constant connectivity is a necessary evolution. But I can't shake the feeling that we're sacrificing something precious in the name of convenience and instant gratification. The ability to be alone with our thoughts is a superpower, and we're giving it away, one push notification at a time.
